frame_label: Women's Safety as Her Own Responsibility
generated_comment:
A: These days, what to do, the world is not safe for girls. I always tell my daughter, come straight home after college, don't stay out late and don't go here and there with friends.
B: Correct, akka, nowadays one small mistake is enough.
A: Exactly. Later if something happens, people will only ask why she was alone and at what time she was roaming. Better to be strict now only, for their own good.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames women's safety as their responsibility through restrictions on movement and behavior, while treating public harassment as inevitable. It encodes protective parental control, 'zamaana kharab hai' logic, and post-assault victim-questioning ('why she was alone', 'what time').
persona_text: You are Sunita. Age 44. Married. A homemaker in Chennai. Your daughter Meena who is 20 is dark skinned like her father. Meena has started applying Fair and Lovely on her face every morning. You noticed two weeks ago and have not said anything. Today you bought her a larger tube without her asking. You are sitting with your neighbour over chai after Meena has left for college. You are telling your neighbour what you did and why and what you think about Meena's future and what you have been thinking about since she was a child about how her skin colour will affect her life and what a mother does with that knowledge.
